summary: Julia Evans recounts running Python workshops for high school girls at PyData NYC, organized by NumFOCUS with Hacker School volunteers. The beginner group learned programming concepts through Ren'Py story games, while the advanced group explored NYC 311 complaint data using Python. Evans emphasizes prioritizing fun over maximizing learning content, noting the students were genuinely engaged with exploring data from their own neighborhoods. The event also featured demos, talks, and a panel where women in tech shared their diverse paths into programming.
key_points: Teaching programming through personally relevant data (students' own ZIP codes and neighborhoods) created genuine engagement and curiosity
Prioritizing enjoyment over cramming maximum content proved to be an effective workshop strategy
The workshops used creative entry points for programming: story games for beginners and data analysis for advanced students
The panel highlighted diverse paths into programming, including people who came from music and other non-technical backgrounds
money_quotes: I tried to work more on making sure they had a good time than making them Learn the Maximum Amount of Things, and I think that went pretty well.
One person found that someone had been complaining about "Unsanitary Pigeon Conditions" near their house.
The colour-changing was more exciting than I expected – they got pretty into investigating which colours matplotlib knew about, and were really impressed.

One person said she studied music and then started programming later, and someone else said she wanted to study music, but her parents made her do something more practical and she ended up enjoying programming.
I am always amazed by how cool the programmer women I meet are.
key_insight: Making programming fun and personally relevant through local data exploration is more effective than trying to maximize the amount of technical content taught.
